title: Why offload to a background job?
prompt_md: Your `POST /checkout` endpoint must charge a slow third-party payment API and then send a confirmation email. Why is it better to enqueue this as a background job rather than doing it all inside the request?
options: Because it keeps the request fast and responsive — the slow, failure-prone work runs asynchronously and can be retried, without blocking or breaking the user's response
Because queues guarantee the work runs exactly once, removing all need for error handling
Because you can no longer charge the customer twice once the work is in a queue
Because background jobs run faster than request handlers do
explanation_md:
The win is **responsiveness and resilience**, not raw speed. The handler does the minimum (enqueue a job) and returns immediately, so the user isn't stuck waiting on a slow payment API and a blocked worker can't take down your whole site. The heavy, flaky work runs in a worker where it can be retried independently of the user's response.

The others are wrong: jobs don't run faster, queues are typically *at-least-once* (not exactly-once, so you still need idempotency/error handling), and using a queue does not by itself prevent double-charges — that's what idempotency is for.
